Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. At 28, I've got a lot going on outside of school - work, bills, life stuff. I'd usually try to squeeze in study time whenever I could, maybe late at night or on weekends when things calmed down. It was pretty chaotic, and I know my 15 hours a week probably got scattered all over the place. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd often just listen to lectures while doing other things around the house.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, to be honest. Deadlines would hit and suddenly I'd be cramming, but then weeks would go by where I barely touched anything. With only 45% attendance, you can tell I wasn't keeping a steady rhythm. Life just kept getting in the way - work shifts, unexpected expenses eating into my time and mental energy. I wanted to be more consistent but it never quite happened.
Q3: Usually it was panic, if I'm being real. I'd get a notification about a deadline or realize I'd missed something, and that scared feeling would push me to log in. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I was falling behind, which I usually was. The fear of failing completely was what drove most of my LMS visits. Occasionally I'd go in because I genuinely wanted to catch up on lecture recordings.
Q4: Not really, no. I tried a few times to make it a habit, like logging in every morning with my coffee, but it never stuck. The LMS felt more like this looming thing I had to deal with rather than something natural in my day. With low resources and limited time, it always felt like an extra burden. I'd go through phases where I'd be on it more, but those never lasted more than a week or two.
Q5: The lecture recordings were my lifeline since I learn best by listening. I could download them or stream them while commuting or doing chores, which fit my life better. The discussion boards were actually helpful too when people explained things in different ways - I posted about 15 times trying to understand stuff. Video content worked well for me because I could replay parts I didn't get. Reading heavy materials were harder for me to engage with.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. My brain just doesn't work well with walls of text - I need to hear things. The optional quizzes and practice exercises I kept putting off because they felt like extra work when I was already struggling to keep up. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later never came. My assignment completion rate shows that pretty clearly.
Q7: If I could see a direct connection to the exam or final grade, I'd do it. Activities that felt like they'd actually help me pass were worth my time. Also, anything I could engage with through listening rather than reading felt more accessible to me. If something seemed like busywork or wasn't clearly connected to assessment, I'd skip it. Time is precious when you're stretched thin like I am.
Q8: Optional stuff felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. When you're barely keeping your head above water with required work, optional activities just fall off the radar completely. Required activities had that fear factor attached - I knew I'd face consequences if I didn't do them. Optional ones, even if they might help, just didn't trigger that same urgency. It's not that I didn't want to do them; I just couldn't justify the time.
Q9: Absolutely, yes. I'd download lecture audio and listen to it on my phone during bus rides to work or while cooking. I had a notebook where I'd jot down key points while listening - old school, but it worked for me. I also used some free YouTube videos to supplement topics I was struggling with. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the computer entirely. The LMS was more like a starting point to get materials.
Q10: There were whole weekends where I'd be reviewing my handwritten notes and listening to downloaded lectures on repeat, but the LMS would show nothing. I'd be walking around my apartment talking through concepts out loud to myself - that's how I process information. Sometimes I'd study with a friend from the course in person, quizzing each other verbally. None of that shows up anywhere in the system, but it's where real learning happened for me.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once on the LMS, download the audio or take notes, and then never go back to the platform for that material. Why would I log in again when I already had what I needed saved locally? My phone became my study device more than my laptop. I'd revisit those downloaded materials multiple times, but the system only saw that one initial access. It was more practical for my lifestyle.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got harder. Work picked up extra shifts, and I was scared about money, which made it hard to focus on school. My stress levels were through the roof, and even though I'm not usually an anxious person, I started feeling this constant dread about falling behind. The CBT sessions helped me process some of that fear, but it didn't magically give me more hours in the day. I felt like I was drowning for a few weeks there.
Q13: Constantly. Fatigue was a big one - after a long shift, the last thing I wanted to do was stare at a screen. Confusion about course material made me avoid logging in because I didn't want to face how lost I was. When bills piled up or family stuff came up, school just had to take a backseat. The LMS became this thing I'd avoid because engaging with it meant confronting how behind I was. It was easier to just not look.
Q14: Yeah, I'd sometimes scroll through materials without really absorbing anything. My eyes would be on the screen but my mind was elsewhere, worrying about other stuff. I'd watch videos at 2x speed just to technically get through them without really learning. There were times I'd have the LMS open in a tab while doing other things, so it looked like I was engaged but I really wasn't. Going through the motions, basically.
Q15: The assignments were the big thing I avoided, which my 2.4% completion rate shows pretty clearly. I knew they were important, but they felt overwhelming, and I kept telling myself I'd catch up later. Fear of doing them badly made me not do them at all, which is stupid when I think about it. I'd procrastinate by doing easier tasks that felt productive but weren't actually moving the needle. It was self-sabotage, honestly.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly find time I didn't know I had. But I was always reactive, never proactive - scrambling at the last minute rather than working steadily. The panic would spike my engagement for a day or two, then I'd crash and disappear again. It wasn't healthy, but it's how I operated. The system probably shows these spikes pretty clearly.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it did affect me. Positive feedback on my exam scores - I got an 80.95 - gave me hope that I wasn't completely hopeless. But feedback on assignments was rare since I barely submitted any. The lack of feedback on my participation made me feel invisible, which made it easier to disengage. I think more regular check-ins might have kept me more accountable, but I also might have found them overwhelming.
Q18: The structure felt like it assumed students had more time and consistency than I did. Ten online courses is a lot to juggle, and the way content was released didn't always match my availability. I appreciated that things were online so I could theoretically access them anytime, but the pacing still felt designed for traditional students. The flexibility was both a blessing and a curse - it let me drift without guardrails.
Q19: Having audio and video content made engagement easier for someone like me who learns by listening. But the heavy reliance on written assignments was harder - I can understand material but struggle to produce written work under pressure. The discussion forums were actually helpful because I could engage in smaller chunks. What made it harder was when everything piled up at once with no clear priority guidance. I needed more structure, not less.
Q20: That could be someone like me who's logging in out of anxiety, checking things repeatedly without actually doing the work. Or someone who's genuinely trying to understand the material but can't translate that into submitted assignments. Fear can make you circle around something without actually tackling it. You might watch every video and read every page but freeze when it comes to producing something. The gap between consuming and creating is real.
Q21: That's literally me. My exam score was over 80, and I got a Merit, but my LMS activity was pretty low. I studied in ways that didn't show up - listening to downloaded content, reviewing notes, talking through concepts. The system sees logins and clicks, not actual learning. Some people just don't need to be constantly on the platform to understand the material. We find other ways that work better for our lives and learning styles.
Q22: Logs can show patterns - when someone's active, what they click on, how long they're on a page. That's useful for spotting who's completely absent. But it misses the quality of engagement, offline studying, and whether someone's actually learning or just going through motions. It can't capture fear, stress, or life circumstances that affect engagement. It's like measuring how often someone opens the fridge without knowing if they're actually eating well.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and understanding the material, not just showing up. It's when your brain is actively working on the content, making connections, asking questions. For me, engagement happened most when I was listening to lectures and things clicked into place. It's not about how many times you log in or how long you spend on a page. Real engagement is internal - it's about whether you're actually learning and growing.
Q24: The fear I carried throughout this course wouldn't show up anywhere. The fact that I'm 28, working, and stretched thin financially - that context is invisible. My offline studying, my auditory learning happening through headphones on the bus, my handwritten notes - none of that registers. The CBT sessions helped me manage my anxiety about falling behind, but that emotional journey isn't in any log. The data shows a disengaged student; the reality is someone fighting hard just to stay afloat.
